chunk_summary: Tele-follow-up for feeding difficulties and faltering growth ensures adherence, rapid troubleshooting, and integration with community systems, focusing on clinically stable children with high adherence risks.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of tele-follow-up in managing feeding difficulties and faltering growth.
Identify criteria for selecting tele-follow-up versus in-person assessment.
Learn how to integrate tele-follow-up data with community programs like ICDS and MCP/RCH records.
key_facts: Tele-follow-up is suitable for clinically stable children with high adherence risks, such as travel barriers or caregiver overload.
Recommended check-in intervals: 48–72 hours for high-risk infants and 7 days for toddlers starting new meal structures.
Minimum dataset includes milk volume (mL/day), meal/snack count, meal duration, and choking/coughing events.
Families can provide a 3-day diary or daily 'before and after' meal photos for adherence verification.
Community integration involves weighing at anganwadi/ICDS platforms using MCP/RCH records to ensure comparable measurements.
reference_quote: Use NICE principles for regular but not excessive monitoring, and maintain safeguarding vigilance when follow-up repeatedly fails despite outreach.
document_key_topics: Community program integration for growth monitoring
